old_claim.note: Botanical Gardens & Herbaria provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Comparative anatomy used knowledge of human anatomy and physiology as the baseline for comparing animal structures and interpreting animal models.

source_shape.source_locator: Springer definition/introduction: comparative anatomy compares human and animal body structures across species, and early modern anatomists used animal comparison to learn more about human anatomy and physiology.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source defines early modern comparative anatomy around human-animal and animal-animal comparison, with human anatomy and physiology as an explicit interpretive target.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing the plant-collection analogy with the human-anatomy prerequisite because the corrected node is about cross-species anatomical comparison rather than botanical collection infrastructure.

ontology_before: Comparative Anatomy Collections depended on Botanical Gardens & Herbaria, implying plant collection and herbarium infrastructure directly enabled early comparative anatomy.
ontology_after: Comparative Anatomy Collections now depend on human anatomy/physiology and scientific illustration printing, keeping the node scoped to cross-species anatomical observation and printed comparison.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: comparative_anatomy_collections no longer directly depends on botanical_gardens_herbaria and now preserves a direct human-anatomy prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to general natural-history cabinet collecting where botanical and zoological specimen systems are the central mechanism.
A stronger source shows botanical gardens or herbaria directly enabled Belon's comparative anatomy rather than serving only as parallel collection institutions.
short_reason: Human-animal anatomical comparison needs human anatomy as its baseline; botanical gardens are not the direct mechanism.
